params.text:
Verbal communication

Verbal communication is the words you use, spoken or written. In care the way you say something often matters as much as the words themselves. A warm, calm tone reassures; a rushed or sharp tone can worry someone even when your words are kind.

Choose plain, respectful language. Avoid jargon, abbreviations and baby talk. Speak clearly and at a steady pace, give the person time to answer, and use their preferred name. Pitch, volume and pace all carry meaning, so stay aware of how you sound, not just what you say. Written notes and messages count as verbal communication too, so keep them clear and easy to read.

Non-verbal communication

Much of what we communicate is non-verbal — without words. Facial expression, eye contact, posture, gestures, touch and personal space all send messages, and they are often believed more readily than the words themselves. People with communication difficulties may rely on these signals heavily, both to understand you and to express themselves.

  • Body language: an open, relaxed posture invites conversation.
  • Facial expression: a genuine smile puts people at ease.
  • Eye contact: shows you are listening, but respect cultural differences.
  • Match your signals: your words and body language should agree.

Watch the other person's non-verbal cues too — they often reveal how someone truly feels, especially when their words and their body language do not seem to match. A person who says they are fine while looking tense may need you to gently check in.

params.question: You are helping someone who seems anxious. Which approach uses non-verbal communication well?

answers.text: Sit at their level, keep a relaxed posture and a warm expression, and give them time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Matching calm body language to your reassuring words helps the person feel safe.

answers.text: Stand over them and speak quickly so the task is finished sooner.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Standing over someone and rushing can feel intimidating and increase their anxiety.

answers.text: Avoid all eye contact so you do not intrude on them.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Some eye contact shows you are listening; avoiding it completely can feel cold or dismissive.

answers.text: Fold your arms and glance at the clock while they speak.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Closed posture and clock-watching signal impatience and discourage the person from talking.
